resume:
Marketing Intern Candidate — Integrated Marketing & Social

Personal statement: I am a rising senior studying Marketing with a minor in Data Analytics, and I am genuinely energized by the intersection of storytelling and measurement. Over the past three years I have deliberately sought out a wide range of hands-on experiences — campus organizations, two prior internships, freelance social work, and a student-run agency — because I believe the only way to learn marketing is to actually run campaigns, watch them succeed or flop, and sit with the data afterward. I am told I am a strong collaborator, and I tend to thrive in cross-functional teams where I can connect the creative side with the analytical side. I am applying for this internship because I want to learn from practitioners who treat marketing as both a craft and a discipline, and because I want to be pushed to be more rigorous about attributing outcomes to actions.

Relevant experience:

State University Marketing Association — VP of Campaigns (2024–present)
This is the largest student professional organization on campus, with roughly 180 members, and as VP of Campaigns I help coordinate our flagship initiatives. The biggest of these is our annual career-fair promotion push, which we run jointly with the career center. Working closely with our president, our design chair, and a committee of about ten members, we plan a multi-week promotional calendar spanning Instagram, our email list, flyering, and classroom announcements. I contribute to the content calendar, help draft captions, and coordinate with the design committee on the visual assets. Last year the fair saw record attendance, which the whole team was thrilled about, and we attributed a good portion of that to the coordinated social push we built together as a committee. I also sit in on our weekly leadership meetings where we review what is working across our channels.

Momentum Digital (boutique agency) — Social Media Intern (Summer 2024)
This was my first agency experience, and it taught me an enormous amount about how real client work gets done under deadline pressure. I was staffed onto a pod supporting three small-business clients — a local fitness studio, a regional coffee roaster, and a boutique hotel. My day-to-day involved drafting social copy, scheduling posts through the agency's social-management tool, pulling weekly performance snapshots, and joining client check-in calls to take notes. The team I worked with was fantastic, and my account lead in particular was a wonderful mentor who walked me through how she thought about content strategy for each brand. Over the summer the coffee-roaster account in particular saw nice engagement growth, and the pod was really proud of that result; it felt great to be part of a team that delivered for a client. I also helped the team put together an end-of-summer recap deck that we presented to the clients.

Freelance — Social Media Helper (2023–2024)
On the side I have done some informal freelance work for a couple of local businesses, mostly a friend's bakery and a small nonprofit. For the bakery I helped set up their Instagram presence and we tried out a few different content styles together with the owner to see what landed. I would not call myself an expert, but it gave me a feel for how much trial and error goes into finding a voice for a brand, and how important it is to actually look at which posts perform rather than just guessing.

State University Student-Run Agency — Account Team Member (2023)
For one semester I was on a four-person account team serving a real local client, a farmers' market, as part of a project-based course. We worked together to build out a small spring-promotion campaign. My contributions included helping with audience research and contributing to the content ideas, and the team collectively presented our recommendations to the client at the end of the term. The client was happy with what we put together, and I learned a lot about how a small team divides up creative and analytical work.

Skills and tools: social-media scheduling tools, basic analytics dashboards, Canva, light Google Sheets analysis, copywriting fundamentals, content-calendar planning, audience research basics. I am comfortable reading an engagement report and I am working on getting more confident with attribution concepts and paid-media metrics, which is one reason this internship appeals to me.

Leadership and involvement: VP of Campaigns for the Marketing Association; volunteer social helper for a campus food-pantry drive; participant in two case competitions where my teams reached the semifinal round.

Education: BBA in Marketing, minor in Data Analytics, expected 2026. Relevant coursework: Marketing Analytics, Consumer Behavior, Digital Marketing, Statistics for Business, Brand Management. GPA on the Dean's List for the last three semesters.

What I am looking for: a summer internship where I can move from being one contributor on a team to actually owning a slice of a campaign end to end, learn to defend my own decisions with data, and be mentored by people who hold a high bar for rigor.
jd: We are hiring a Marketing Intern for our integrated marketing team for a 12-week summer program. You will be embedded with our social and lifecycle marketers and given ownership of a scoped piece of a live campaign — not just support tasks. We expect interns to be able to point to their own specific contribution and the decisions they personally made, and to begin reasoning about how a given action moved a metric. The strongest candidates can separate what the team did from what they individually owned, and can talk about a result they are personally accountable for. You will run at least one small experiment, analyze the outcome, and present it. We care about curiosity, intellectual honesty, and a willingness to take ownership over hiding behind the group.

history.q: Tell me about a marketing project you've been part of that you're proud of.
On that career-fair push, what specifically did you own versus what the committee owned?
You said attendance hit a record. What did you personally do that you'd tie to that result?

candidate_last_answer: Yeah, so the career-fair promotion was a real team effort — we all pitched in. The committee built the content calendar together, we collaborated on the captions, and the design chair and I worked alongside everyone on the visuals. As a group we decided to lean harder into Instagram that year, and we were all really proud when attendance hit a record. I'd say it came down to how well we coordinated as a team across all the channels — everyone played a part and it really showed in the turnout.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: everyone played a part and it really showed in the turnout

expected-yield: one specific task the candidate personally executed or decided on the career-fair push (e.g. a particular post, segment, or scheduling call they made themselves) and any signal linking it to attendance, rather than crediting the committee as a whole
